topics.title: Locks & Concurrency Utilities — ReentrantLock, ReadWriteLock, Semaphore
topics.description: java.util.concurrent.locks provides explicit lock objects with more control than synchronized. ReentrantLock adds tryLock(), timed lock, interruptible lock, and fairness. ReadWriteLock allows concurrent reads but exclusive writes — critical for read-heavy caches. Semaphore limits concurrent access to N threads. StampedLock (Java 8) adds optimistic reads. These primitives underpin thread-safe caches, connection pools, and rate limiters.
topics.story: synchronized is like a single bathroom key — one person at a time, no questions asked. ReentrantLock is a smart key box with a timer: you can ask 'give me the key, but only wait 5 seconds' (tryLock). ReadWriteLock is a library: many people can read books simultaneously (read lock), but only one can rearrange shelves (write lock) and everyone waits. Semaphore is a parking lot with N spaces — you wait at the barrier until a space opens.
topics.analogy: ReentrantLock = upgraded synchronized + tryLock + Condition variables. ReadWriteLock: readLock() = shared access (many can hold simultaneously). writeLock() = exclusive (all others blocked). Semaphore(N): N permits — acquire() takes one, release() returns one. Counts down to 0 then blocks. CountDownLatch: one-time gate — count down to zero, all waiting threads released. CyclicBarrier: reusable checkpoint — all N threads wait at the barrier, then proceed together.
topics.memoryAnchor: ReentrantLock: lock() / unlock() in finally. tryLock(timeout, unit) returns boolean. lockInterruptibly(). new ReentrantLock(true) = fair. ReadWriteLock rwl = new ReentrantReadWriteLock(); rwl.readLock().lock(); rwl.writeLock().lock(). Semaphore(N): acquire() / release(). CountDownLatch(N): await() / countDown(). CyclicBarrier(N, action): await(). Condition: lock.newCondition(); cond.await(); cond.signal();
topics.firstPrinciples: synchronized uses object monitor (intrinsic lock) — cannot be interrupted, timed, or tested without blocking. ReentrantLock uses CAS (Compare-And-Swap) internally via AbstractQueuedSynchronizer (AQS). Fairness prevents starvation but reduces throughput. ReadWriteLock upgrade (read → write) is not supported — must release read lock first. Semaphore is also AQS-based — permits are an atomic counter.
topics.bruteForce: Use synchronized everywhere — simple but coarse-grained. Read-heavy cache with synchronized: all readers block each other unnecessarily. Rate limiting with synchronized counter: polling loop wastes CPU.
topics.optimizedApproach: Read-heavy cache: ReadWriteLock — concurrent reads, exclusive writes. Connection pool: Semaphore — limit concurrent DB connections. Startup coordination: CountDownLatch — wait for N services ready. Parallel batch: CyclicBarrier — sync N threads at checkpoint. Timed operations: tryLock(timeout) instead of blocking forever.
topics.whenToUse: ReentrantLock over synchronized: when you need tryLock, timed lock, interruptible lock, or multiple Condition variables. ReadWriteLock: caches, configuration stores (few writes, many reads). Semaphore: connection/thread pools, rate limiting. CountDownLatch: wait for initialization to complete (one-time). CyclicBarrier: parallel computation phases (reusable). Prefer higher-level concurrent collections (ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList) before reaching for explicit locks.

examples.title: ReentrantLock with tryLock & Condition Variables
examples.explanation: Condition variables split the single wait-set of synchronized into two named wait queues — notFull for producers, notEmpty for consumers. This avoids spurious wake-ups and prevents producer from waking another producer (only notEmpty.signal() wakes consumers). tryLock with timeout is the standard pattern to avoid deadlock: if you can't get both locks quickly, release what you have and retry with backoff.
examples.realWorldUse: Java LinkedBlockingQueue uses exactly this pattern internally. DevLearner code execution: BoundedQueue as a submission queue limiting concurrent code runs to N. Connection pool: acquire() blocks if no connections available — tryLock with timeout throws after N seconds rather than blocking forever.

examples.title: ReadWriteLock Cache & Semaphore Rate Limiter
examples.explanation: ReadWriteLock allows N concurrent readers when no writer holds the lock. A write acquisition waits for all active readers to finish. This is optimal for caches: 99% reads proceed in parallel, 1% writes serialize. Semaphore with fair=true prevents starvation — threads acquire permits in arrival order. tryAcquire with timeout is essential in production to avoid hanging forever if the pool is exhausted.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring @Cacheable uses ConcurrentHashMap under the hood — similar concurrency model. HikariCP (connection pool) uses Semaphore internally. Nginx rate limiting, API gateway throttling (N requests/second) use Semaphore semantics. DevLearner: cache topic metadata with ReadWriteLock — frequent reads, rare admin writes.

problems.title: Thread-Safe Counter with ReentrantLock
problems.hint1: Always call unlock() in a finally block.
problems.hint2: A single ReentrantLock protects all three methods.
problems.hint3: Use ExecutorService to launch 10 threads each doing 100 increments, then assert count == 1000.

problems.title: Read-Write Cache with ReadWriteLock
problems.description:
Implement a read-write cache using `ReentrantReadWriteLock`: concurrent reads, exclusive writes.

**Example:**
ReadWriteLock rwLock = new ReentrantReadWriteLock();
get(key): rwLock.readLock().lock() // multiple threads can read simultaneously
put(key,val): rwLock.writeLock().lock() // exclusive — all readers block

100 reader threads + 1 writer: readers proceed concurrently; writer gets exclusive access

**Key insight:** Read-write lock doubles throughput for read-heavy workloads vs pure `synchronized`.
problems.hint1: readLock() for get(), writeLock() for put() and remove().
problems.hint2: Always unlock in finally blocks — use separate lock/unlock for read and write paths.
problems.hint3: Multiple threads calling get() simultaneously should not block each other.
problems.sampleOutput: reads all proceed concurrently; write is exclusive

problems.title: Semaphore-based Resource Pool
problems.description:
Implement a database connection pool using `Semaphore(maxConnections)`.

**Example:**
Semaphore semaphore = new Semaphore(3); // 3 max connections
ConnectionPool pool = new ConnectionPool(3);
pool.acquire() x3 => success
pool.acquire() => blocks until one is released
pool.release(conn) => unblocks the waiting thread

**Key insight:** Semaphore permits = available connections. `acquire()` blocks when 0 permits remain; `release()` adds a permit.
problems.hint1: Semaphore(N) limits concurrent access to N. acquire() blocks when count reaches 0.
problems.hint2: Use a ConcurrentLinkedQueue to hold the available resources.
problems.hint3: acquire() → poll from queue. release() → add back to queue + semaphore.release().
problems.testCases:
Got: R1, R2
After release, got: R1
Done
